figures.caption: Table 3: Comparison of COAST against existing VLA adaptation paradigms. COAST is the only method that achieves holistic, multi-dimensional steering without requiring gradient updates, external models, or computationally expensive test-time search.

Table 11: Positive-only conceptor steering on 18 high-baseline MetaWorld-ML45 tasks. Success rates are over 15 rollouts. “Best Pos-Only” is the maximum across six configurations (α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1.0}, β = 0.2, global vs. per-step). Bold entries mark strict improvements over baseline.

Table 1: Contrastive conceptors deliver the largest and most consistent gains across every model–benchmark pair. We evaluate three conceptor steering strategies (+Glob., +Per., +Pos.) defined in Section 3.2 and compare against +CAA (linear contrastive activation addition), a finetuning baseline (+SFT), and a +SAE baseline (TopK SAE feature selection; per-task best α over the grid {0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0}) (Khan et al., 2025). LIBERO-10 task names describe the manipulated objects (e.g., Stove+Moka = turn on stove and place moka pot; full names in App. A.8). ∆: mean gain over unsteered baseline. pt: paired t-test across tasks; pz: pooled two-proportion z-test. ∗p < 0.05, ∗∗p < 0.01, ∗∗∗p < 0.001. Bold: best method per row; underline: second-best. †The Cheese+Butter task has zero failures under the π0-FAST checkpoint, so no contrastive conceptor can be fitted; means and ∆ for that column are over 9 contrastive-eligible tasks. (Right) Real-robot success rates on three manipulation tasks. We compare π0.5+COAST, π0.5, π0-FAST, and GR00T on 3 three real-world robot tasks.

Table 15: Oracle gap analysis. Geometric selection achieves 86–93% of oracle performance while evaluating only a small fraction of the full configuration grid. Selected parameters: GR00T N1.5 RoboCasa uses ℓ=10, α=0.1; π0.5 RoboCasa uses ℓ=11, α=0.5; π0.5 LIBERO uses ℓ=11, α ∈ {0.5, 1.0}. All benchmarks use β ∈ {0.1, 0.3}.
Table 14: Hyperparameter sweep grid per model–benchmark combination.

Figure 7: Three-stage hyperparameter selection, validated on LIBERO-10 with π0.5. (A) Quota q(C) across layers (boxes) and mean steered success (red) both peak at L=11, so quota alone identifies the correct layer. (B) Mean success versus overlap at L=11. Success rises as overlap decreases, then saturates. (C) Overlap decreases monotonically with aperture α, so selecting the overlap band determines a narrow α range with no rollouts. (D) Success heatmap over α × β at L=11, confirming that small β values perform comparably, leaving 2–3 configurations to evaluate per task.
Table 20: Filtered-BC hyperparameters. Identical across MetaWorld, LIBERO, and RoboCasa.

Figure 5: Tasks share failure geometry but not success geometry, enabling cross-task steering. Left: Joint PCA of layer-11 activations on π0.5 LIBERO, 2σ ellipses per task. Failure activations (top) spread into task-specific regions whose overlap varies; success activations (bottom) cluster uniformly across tasks. Right: Each dot is one source→target pair on LIBERO or RoboCasa. Failure-subspace containment tr(Cf srcC f tgt)/tr((C f src) 2) correlates with transfer success-rate gain (top; r=0.30 and 0.49, p<0.005); success containment does not (bottom; |r|<0.13, p>0.4).

Figure 1: Overview of COAST. COAST steers a frozen robot policy at inference time by multiplicatively gating its residual stream with a contrastive conceptor fit from rollout activations. Offline, success and failure rollouts yield layer-ℓ activations, from which closed-form conceptors C+ and C− are fit and composed via Boolean subspace algebra into Csteer = C+ ∧ ¬C−. Inference, the residual h at layer ℓ is gated by M = (1 − β)I + β Csteer to produce h′ = hM⊤, which flows through the rest of the frozen model. No gradient computation or weight modification is required.

Figure 8: Successful Real World Rollouts of π0.5-COAST. We evaluate on three different tasks: Open Drawer, Close Microwave, and Put Duck in Cabinet over 15 independent trials per task. This is an example trajectory of COAST in each of the tasks.

Table 7: Conceptor steering results of π0.5 on MetaWorld ML45 (all 26 steered tasks). For each task and steering strategy, we report the best success rate over a hyperparameter sweep of α ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1.0} and β ∈ {0.1, 0.3, 0.5} at layer 11. Global: contrastive conceptor (Csucc∧¬Cfail) applied uniformly across denoise steps. Per-Step: contrastive conceptor applied independently at each denoise step. Pos.-Only: success conceptor without failure subtraction. Best hyperparameters shown in parentheses. Each condition is evaluated over 30 held-out test rollouts.

Table 8: Conceptor steering results for π0-FAST on MetaWorld ML45 (all 27 steered tasks). For each task and steering strategy, we report the best success rate over an extended hyperparameter sweep at layer 17. The grids are αglobal ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1, 2, 10}, αper-step ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1}, αpos.-only ∈ {0.1, 0.5, 1, 2}, and β ∈ {0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.5}. Global: contrastive conceptor (Csucc ∧ ¬Cfail) applied uniformly across denoise steps. Per-Step: contrastive conceptor applied independently at each denoise step. Pos.-Only: success conceptor without failure subtraction. Best hyperparameters shown in parentheses. Each condition is evaluated over 16 rollouts. Bold marks values strictly above the unsteered baseline.
Table 4: Conceptor-based activation steering results of π0.5 on LIBERO-10 with layer sweep in ∈ {5, 11}. Each condition is evaluated over 30 held-out test rollouts.

Table 5: Conceptor-based activation steering results of π0-FAST on LIBERO-10. LR2 (cream cheese & butter) has nfail = 0 under this checkpoint, so contrastive conceptors (Global, Per-Step) cannot be constructed; means are computed over the 9 contrastive-eligible tasks.Each condition is evaluated over 30 held-out test rollouts.
Table 6: Conceptor-based activation steering results of π0.5 on RoboCasa with layer sweep ∈ {5, 11}. Each condition is evaluated over 30 held-out test rollouts.

Figure 4: Conceptor steering pulls activations toward the success region. (A) Per-step activations projected onto the top two eigenvectors of Csteer, the subspace maximally separating baseline success from failure. Solid ellipses: baseline 2σ regions; dashed: steered; large markers: centroids. (B) Projection onto v1(Csteer) over normalized trajectory time (means ±1σ).

Table 2: Transfer experiment across benchmarks. For each target task, we report the best selfsteered result (Self) and the top-2 transfer sources. Each transfer cell shows source (strategy) SR delta, indicating the change in success rate compared to the target task’s unsteered baseline. G = global strategy, PS = per-step strategy. Values with SR delta ≥ +0.50 are shown in bold. LIBERO task names follow Table 1.
Table 17: Per-task oracle-selected hyperparameters for pi0.5 LIBERO-10.

Table 9: Conceptor-based activation steering results on RoboCasa with GR00T N1.5, showing the selected layer, aperture α, and mixing weight β for the best configuration of each strategy. Bold indicates the best performance. Each condition is evaluated over 30 held-out test rollouts.

Table 10: Contrastive conceptors outperform both linear additive steering and randomdirection controls on every cell where both baselines were run. For each task we report the best success rate over a hyperparameter sweep (15 rollouts per cell). Global: contrastive conceptor Csucc ∧ ¬Cfail, shown as a reference for the main method (full results in Table 1). Linear: additive activation steering using the difference of mean success and failure activations. Random: random-direction conceptor with matched eigenvalue spectrum but random orthonormal eigenvectors, controlling for spectral filtering shape. Bold marks the best of the two ablated baselines per row.
